%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
## Overview

> **Objective:** Understand how matrices can be added, subtracted, and multiplied — and know exactly when each operation is allowed.

Section B.6-2 introduces the arithmetic of matrices: the rules for combining them through addition, subtraction, and multiplication. Unlike ordinary numbers, matrix operations come with strict **dimension requirements** — you cannot add two matrices of different sizes, and multiplication is only defined when the inner dimensions match. After working through this section, you will be able to check whether two matrices are conformable for a given operation, carry out the operation correctly, and recognize the key properties (such as 
\(AI = IA = A\) and \(|AB| = |A||B|\)) that appear repeatedly on exams.

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="generate_image" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
*🎨 Three core matrix operations at a glance: addition (same size), multiplication (inner dims match), and the identity property.*
![Illustration](/generated/gptimage2-1777215650091-6306.png)

## Core Idea

The central relationship in matrix algebra is the **dimension compatibility rule**: every operation has a size requirement, and violating it makes the operation undefined.

**Addition** is the simplest case. Two matrices \(A\) and \(B\) can be added only if they have the **same order** \(m \times n\). The result is computed element-by-element:

$$c_{ij} = a_{ij} + b_{ij}$$

**Multiplication** is more subtle. For \(AB\) to be defined, the number of **columns of \(A\)** must equal the number of **rows of \(B\)**. If \(A\) is \(m \times p\) and \(B\) is \(p \times n\), then \(C = AB\) is \(m \times n\), with each entry:

$$c_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_{ik}\, b_{kj}$$

### EXAM TRAP

Matrix multiplication is **not commutative**: \(AB \neq BA\) in general. Many exam questions exploit this. Also remember \(AI = IA = A\), but the identity matrix \(I\) must have the correct order to be conformable.

**Quick example:** If \(A\) is \(2 \times 3\) and \(B\) is \(3 \times 4\), then \(AB\) is \(2 \times 4\). But \(BA\) is **undefined** because \(4 \neq 2\).

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- Matrix addition requires both matrices to have the same order \(m \times n\); add element-by-element.
- Matrix multiplication \(AB\) requires inner dimensions to match; result is \(m \times n\) from \((m \times p)(p \times n)\).
- Multiplication is not commutative: \(AB \neq BA\) in general; \(AI = IA = A\) always holds.

*In the next section we will examine the transpose and inverse of a matrix — two operations that unlock solving systems of linear equations and appear throughout signal processing.*
